turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has our opinion this morning in case 16-309, Maslenjak versus United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: A federal criminal statute, §1425, prohibits -- and I'm quoting here, “Procuring contrary to law and naturalization.” When someone is convicted of that crime, her naturalization, meaning her citizenship is revoked. This case is about what it means for a person to procure her naturalization contrary to law under that statute. Divna Maslenjak and her family came to the United States as Bosnian refugees. Some -- some years later, Maslenjak applied for U.S. citizenship. As part of the application, she swore that she had never lied to government officials to gain entry into this country. Her application was successful and she became a citizen. Soon though, it came out that she had lied when she got her refugee status in the first place. Specifically, she had misrepresented her husband's role in the Bosnian Civil War. So, she also lied when she told naturalization officials that she had never lied and passed immigration proceedings. In other words, she lied about lying and everyone here agrees that lying in naturalization proceedings is against the law. Based on those facts, the government charged Maslenjak with procuring contrary to law of naturalization in violation of §1425 and the Jury convicted. The question here is just what the Jury had to find to reach that result. The government says it was enough if Maslenjak broke the law in the course of procuring naturalization, whether or not her legal violation had any bearing on the decision to grant her citizenship. But we think more was required. A person procures contrary to law her naturalization only when an illegal act contributed to her acquisition of citizenship. We reached that conclusion based on §1425's text; the phrase procures contrary to law of naturalization really just means, obtained citizenship illegally. And for someone to get something illegally, her illegal act has to contribute to her getting it. Suppose someone said to you, “Jane obtained that painting illegally,” but her illegal acts didn't play any role in her obtaining it. You wouldn't have a clue what that person was talking about. You would think that was some kind of riddle. That's because, if no illegal act contributed to getting the painting, then the painting wouldn't have been gotten illegally. And the same goes for citizenship. If an unlawful act here lying in the naturalization process had no cause or relationship to the defendant's obtaining naturalization, then the defendant didn't obtain her naturalization contrary to law. She may be guilty of lying, but she isn't guilty of unlawful procurement of citizenship under §1425. Doing away with this causal requirement would open the door to a world of strange consequences, ones we'd need far stronger textual support to believe Congress intended. Suppose for instance, that a person completing the naturalization forms fails to disclose a prior speeding violation. Under the government's view, her citizenship could be taken away on that meager basis even years after the fact because she broke the law in the course of getting citizenship. The text of §1425 just doesn't suggest that Congress meant to go that far. Now, although the statute always requires the government to prove a causal relationship, the government can carry that burden in different ways, in different cases. Sometimes, it would just show that the fact a person lied about may be a felony conviction in the past would have made her ineligible for citizenship if the truth had been known. In that case, the causal relationship is obvious. Other times, the government can prove that the defendant's lie obstructed the government's investigation of the defendant and that in unhindered investigation would predictably have unearthed a legal basis for denying citizenship. We read §1425 to allow that kind of proof of a causal connection as well. Here though, the Jury was told that it didn't have to find any kind of causal relationship between Maslenjak's unlawful lie and her getting naturalization, because those Jury instructions were faulty, we vacate the judgment below. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment in which Justice Thomas has joined. Justice Alito has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment.
